network.overall_strategy: This network constructs L-threonine (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanoic acid) via convergent prebiotic pathways centered on three key hubs: formaldehyde (C1 building block), methylglyoxal (C3 aldol/dehydration product), and HCN (nitrogen carrier). The primary terminal route is a Strecker synthesis from lactaldehyde (2-hydroxypropanal), which correctly yields threonine's carbon skeleton with the appropriate hydroxyl and amino substituents. Upstream diversity arises from: (i) two independent formaldehyde sources (hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis and surface UV photocatalysis on TiO₂), (ii) two independent HCN sources (formamide thermolysis and NH₃+CO photochemistry), (iii) two routes from glyceraldehyde to methylglyoxal (direct dehydration and isomerization via dihydroxyacetone), and (iv) an alternative glycolaldehyde source from pyruvate-derived acetaldehyde photooxidation. Environmental flow is strictly unidirectional: Hydrothermal products (formate, CO, formaldehyde, pyruvate) feed into Surface chemistry (formose, Strecker precursors) which feeds into Biochemical assembly (Strecker synthesis, hydrolysis). No reaction requires transport back from Surface to Hydrothermal.

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite
reactions.mechanism: CO₂ adsorbs on the Fe₃S₄ surface, is activated by electron transfer from the mineral, and undergoes hydrogenation to formate via a surface-bound carboxyl intermediate. The [Fe₄S₄]-like cluster topology of greigite provides the catalytic site analogous to modern carbon monoxide dehydrogenase.
reactions.reasoning: Experimentally demonstrated by Roldan et al. (2015) who showed greigite catalyzes CO₂ reduction to formate, methanol, and pyruvate under simulated hydrothermal conditions. Preiner et al. (2020) confirmed native iron and iron sulfides reduce CO₂ to formate at 100°C under modest H₂ pressures. This is one of the most robust prebiotic carbon fixation reactions.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift reaction

conditions.other: black smoker vent conditions, high temperature zone

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ + H₂ ⇌ CO + H₂O. This equilibrium is shifted toward CO at elevated temperatures (>450K). Magnetite provides the catalytic surface for dissociative H₂ adsorption and subsequent CO₂ reduction.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift reaction is thermodynamically favored at high temperatures and is well-documented in hydrothermal systems. Seewald et al. (2006) demonstrated CO formation from CO₂ reduction in hydrothermal fluids. McCollom & Seewald (2003) showed this reaction operates efficiently on iron oxide surfaces at 250-350°C.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type formaldehyde synthesis
reactions.mechanism: CO chemisorbs on the Fe₃O₄/Fe⁰ surface, undergoes partial hydrogenation through surface hydride species to form a surface-bound formyl intermediate (HCO*), which is further hydrogenated and released as formaldehyde (HCHO). Formaldehyde is the first stable C1 oxygenate in FTT chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: McCollom et al. (1999) and McCollom & Seewald (2006) demonstrated FTT synthesis of oxygenated C1-C3 compounds from CO + H₂ on magnetite at 250°C and 250 bar. Formaldehyde is a kinetically accessible intermediate before further reduction to methanol. Holm & Hennet (1992) proposed FTT as a major source of prebiotic organics at hydrothermal vents.

reactions.name: CO₂ fixation to pyruvate on native iron

conditions.other: mildly acidic to neutral pH, N₂ atmosphere

reactions.mechanism: Multiple CO₂ molecules are sequentially reduced and coupled on the Fe⁰ surface. Fe⁰ serves as both electron donor (oxidized to Fe²⁺) and catalytic surface. C1 units are assembled into C2 (acetate-like) then C3 (pyruvate) through surface-mediated C-C bond formation, analogous to a non-enzymatic reductive acetyl-CoA pathway.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2017, 2019) demonstrated that Fe⁰ and other native metals promote CO₂ fixation to pyruvate and other α-keto acids under mild hydrothermal conditions (100°C). Varma et al. (2018) showed similar fixation pathways. This represents a geochemical analog of the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway and reductive TCA cycle.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: mildly acidic pH enhances decarboxylation rate

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes non-oxidative thermal decarboxylation: CH₃COCOO⁻ → CH₃CHO + CO₂. The pyrite surface stabilizes the transition state and facilitates proton transfer. The reaction proceeds through a cyclic transition state involving the α-keto acid moiety.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of α-keto acids is well-established in prebiotic chemistry. Cooper et al. (2011) and Novikov & Copley (2013) demonstrated pyruvate decarboxylation under mild hydrothermal conditions. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable (ΔG < 0) and proceeds readily at temperatures above 100°C. Cody et al. (2004) studied pyruvate transformations on FeS surfaces.

reactions.name: Condensation of formate and ammonia to formamide

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling in evaporitic pool, concentration by evaporation drives condensation

reactions.mechanism: During the drying phase, formate and ammonia are concentrated on the montmorillonite clay surface. Dehydrative condensation occurs: HCOO⁻ + NH₄⁺ → HCONH₂ + H₂O. The clay surface provides Lewis acid sites (Al³⁺) that activate the carboxylate toward nucleophilic attack by ammonia.
reactions.reasoning: Saladino et al. (2012) identified formamide as a key prebiotic intermediate accessible from formate and ammonia under drying conditions. Bada et al. (2007) discussed formamide accumulation in evaporitic environments. Montmorillonite is well-documented as a catalyst for condensation reactions (Ferris, 2005).

reactions.name: Thermal and UV decomposition of formamide to HCN

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm), dry mineral surface

reactions.mechanism: Formamide adsorbed on TiO₂ surface undergoes UV-assisted dehydration: HCONH₂ → HCN + H₂O. TiO₂ generates electron-hole pairs under UV that catalyze the elimination. Alternatively, thermal decomposition at >200°C also produces HCN.
reactions.reasoning: Saladino et al. (2012, 2015) extensively studied formamide decomposition on mineral surfaces including TiO₂ and showed it produces HCN along with nucleobases and other prebiotic molecules. Barks et al. (2010) confirmed HCN release from heated formamide. This provides the critical nitrogen nucleophile for Strecker synthesis.

reactions.name: Photocatalytic CO₂ reduction to formaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation, shallow aqueous pool, anoxic atmosphere

reactions.mechanism: TiO₂ absorbs UV photons generating electron-hole pairs. The conduction band electrons reduce dissolved CO₂ through sequential 2e⁻ transfers: CO₂ → HCOOH → HCHO. Sulfide minerals (stibnite) act as hole scavengers, preventing back-oxidation and improving quantum yield. Water serves as the ultimate electron donor.
reactions.reasoning: Inoue et al. (1979) first demonstrated photocatalytic CO₂ reduction to formaldehyde on semiconductor surfaces. Guzman & Martin (2009) confirmed UV-driven reduction of CO₂ to formaldehyde in prebiotic surface conditions. This provides an independent formaldehyde source complementary to hydrothermal FTT synthesis, adding pathway redundancy.

reactions.name: UV photocatalytic HCN synthesis from NH₃ and CO

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 280 nm), gas-phase or thin film on mineral surface

reactions.mechanism: NH₃ and CO adsorb on TiO₂ surface. UV-generated reactive species facilitate N-H bond activation and C-N bond formation: NH₃ + CO → HCN + H₂O. The reaction proceeds through an isocyanic acid (HNCO) or formamide intermediate, with photolytic dehydration completing the conversion to HCN.
reactions.reasoning: Abelson (1966) demonstrated HCN formation from NH₃ + CO mixtures under UV irradiation. Tian et al. (2011) modeled photochemical HCN production in early Earth's atmosphere from nitrogen-carbon gas mixtures. Ferris & Hagan (1984) showed HCN production from UV irradiation of reduced gas mixtures. This provides an independent HCN source that does not require the formate-formamide pathway.

reactions.name: Formose reaction: formaldehyde self-aldol to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline pH ~10-11, borate stabilizes intermediates against degradation

reactions.mechanism: Umpolung-type: one formaldehyde molecule is activated to a carbanion-equivalent (Breslow's 1,2-enediol mechanism) which attacks a second formaldehyde as electrophile, forming glycolaldehyde. Borate complexation with the product protects it from further reaction and degradation. This is the initiation step of the formose reaction.
reactions.reasoning: Breslow (1959) proposed the autocatalytic mechanism of the formose reaction. Ricardo et al. (2004) demonstrated that borate minerals stabilize glycolaldehyde and other sugars, channeling the formose reaction toward specific products. Benner et al. (2012) emphasized the role of borate in making the formose reaction prebiotically viable by preventing tar formation.

reactions.name: UV photooxidation of acetaldehyde to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation, shallow pool, anoxic but with TiO₂-generated hydroxyl radicals

reactions.mechanism: TiO₂ photocatalysis generates hydroxyl radical equivalents (from water oxidation by photogenerated holes). These abstract an H atom from the methyl group of acetaldehyde to form •CH₂CHO radical, which is then hydroxylated by a second OH• to form HOCH₂CHO (glycolaldehyde). Net: CH₃CHO + [O] → HOCH₂CHO.
reactions.reasoning: Guzmán et al. (2006) demonstrated TiO₂-mediated photooxidation of simple organics in prebiotic surface environments. The selective α-C-H hydroxylation of aldehydes by radical mechanisms is well-known in atmospheric and aqueous-phase photochemistry. This provides an alternative glycolaldehyde source from hydrothermal acetaldehyde, connecting pyruvate-derived C2 fragments to the formose sugar pathway.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline pH, borate complexation stabilizes glyceraldehyde product

reactions.mechanism: Base-catalyzed aldol reaction: glycolaldehyde is deprotonated at C1 to form an enolate, which attacks formaldehyde as electrophile. The resulting C3 aldol product is glyceraldehyde. Borate complexation with the cis-diol of glyceraldehyde shifts the equilibrium toward product by removing it from the reactive pool.
reactions.reasoning: This is a well-established step in the formose reaction network. Kim et al. (2011) demonstrated selective formation of glyceraldehyde from formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde in the presence of borate. Benner et al. (2012) showed borate channeling selects for specific sugar products. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable under alkaline conditions.

reactions.name: Dehydration of glyceraldehyde to methylglyoxal

conditions.other: mildly acidic to neutral pH, proceeds spontaneously at elevated temperature

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes acid-catalyzed β-elimination of water: HOCH₂CH(OH)CHO → CH₃COCHO + H₂O. The clay surface provides Brønsted acid sites that protonate the C3-OH, facilitating elimination. This is a well-known degradation pathway of glyceraldehyde in aqueous solution at moderate temperatures.
reactions.reasoning: Fedoroňko & Königstein (1969) demonstrated spontaneous dehydration of glyceraldehyde to methylglyoxal in aqueous solution. De Bruijn et al. (1987) confirmed this transformation occurs readily at 50-100°C. Montmorillonite acid sites catalyze dehydration reactions (Ferris, 2005). Methylglyoxal is a thermodynamically stable C3 dicarbonyl that accumulates as a degradation product of trioses.

reactions.name: Isomerization of glyceraldehyde to dihydroxyacetone

conditions.other: neutral to mildly basic pH, Lobry de Bruyn-van Ekenstein rearrangement

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes keto-enol tautomerism via the Lobry de Bruyn-van Ekenstein transformation: the C2-OH is oxidized to a ketone while the C1-aldehyde is reduced to an alcohol, mediated by a 1,2-enediol intermediate. The clay surface facilitates proton transfers.
reactions.reasoning: The Lobry de Bruyn-van Ekenstein transformation between glyceraldehyde and dihydroxyacetone is a classic carbohydrate isomerization well-documented since the late 19th century. Nagorski & Richard (2001) studied the kinetics in aqueous solution. This equilibrium provides an alternative route to methylglyoxal via DHA elimination.

reactions.name: Elimination of water from dihydroxyacetone to methylglyoxal

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite, apatite surface (phosphate as base/acid catalyst)

reactions.mechanism: Dihydroxyacetone undergoes elimination of water from one of its hydroxymethyl groups: HOCH₂COCH₂OH → CH₃COCHO + H₂O. The phosphate surface acts as a bifunctional acid-base catalyst, abstracting a proton from one methylene while the clay protonates the departing hydroxyl. This is the well-known DHA → methylglyoxal conversion.
reactions.reasoning: The dehydration of dihydroxyacetone to methylglyoxal is well-established in biochemistry (the methylglyoxal pathway) and in sugar degradation chemistry. Richard (1993) studied the non-enzymatic elimination kinetics. Thornalley (1996) reviewed methylglyoxal formation from triose sugars. This provides a parallel route to methylglyoxal independent of the direct glyceraldehyde dehydration.

reactions.name: Selective keto-reduction of methylglyoxal to lactaldehyde

conditions.other: anaerobic surface pool rich in dissolved Fe²⁺, pH 6-8, dissolved H₂ as terminal reductant

reactions.mechanism: Methylglyoxal (CH₃COCHO) adsorbs on the FeS surface via coordination of the keto carbonyl oxygen to Fe²⁺ sites. The keto group (C2=O) is selectively reduced by surface hydride (from H₂ dissociation on FeS) to give the secondary alcohol, yielding CH₃CH(OH)CHO (lactaldehyde). Selectivity arises because the keto carbonyl is more electrophilic than the aldehyde when coordinated to Lewis acid Fe²⁺ sites, and the keto group adsorbs preferentially in a bidentate mode with the adjacent aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: FeS minerals are well-established as mild, selective reducing agents in prebiotic chemistry (Heinen & Lauwers, 1996). Selectivity for keto vs. aldehyde reduction on metal sulfide surfaces is rationalized by preferential bidentate coordination of the α-dicarbonyl motif. Huber & Wächtershäuser (2006) demonstrated selective reductions on iron sulfide surfaces. The mild reducing power of FeS/Fe²⁺ at ambient temperature favors partial reduction. Note: this reaction occurs in an anaerobic surface pool, not requiring transport back to hydrothermal vents.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde + HCN + NH₃ → threonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: concentrated prebiotic pool, pH 7-9, room temperature to mildly warm

reactions.mechanism: Step 1: NH₃ attacks the aldehyde carbon of lactaldehyde (CH₃CH(OH)CHO) to form a carbinolamine, which dehydrates to an imine (Schiff base): CH₃CH(OH)CH=NH. Step 2: HCN adds to the electrophilic imine carbon to form the α-aminonitrile: CH₃CH(OH)CH(NH₂)CN (threonine aminonitrile). The existing β-hydroxyl group of lactaldehyde is preserved throughout, ensuring the correct threonine carbon skeleton with the 3-hydroxy substituent.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is one of the most robust and well-studied prebiotic amino acid syntheses. Miller (1957) and Miller & Urey (1959) identified it as a key route to amino acids. Cleaves (2008) reviewed Strecker synthesis under various prebiotic conditions. Critically, the use of lactaldehyde (2-hydroxypropanal) as the aldehyde substrate correctly produces threonine's 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanoic acid skeleton, as the β-hydroxyl is already present in the starting aldehyde and is not affected by the C1-amine/nitrile chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.other: mildly acidic to neutral pH, prolonged aqueous incubation or mild heating

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group (-C≡N) of threonine aminonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: (1) nucleophilic addition of water to the nitrile gives an amide intermediate (threonine amide): CH₃CH(OH)CH(NH₂)CONH₂, (2) further hydrolysis of the amide releases NH₃ and yields the free carboxylic acid: CH₃CH(OH)CH(NH₂)COOH (threonine). Net: -CN + 2H₂O → -COOH + NH₃.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids is thermodynamically favorable and kinetically accessible under mild aqueous conditions. Commeyras et al. (2002) demonstrated aminonitrile hydrolysis at 25-80°C in aqueous solution. The reaction is accelerated by mild heating, acid, or base catalysis. The NH₃ released can be recycled into subsequent Strecker reactions. This completes the conversion of the Strecker aminonitrile to the free amino acid threonine.

pathways.name: FTT-formaldehyde → formose → glyceraldehyde dehydration → Strecker (formamide HCN)
pathways.description: Hydrothermal FTT provides formaldehyde; formose chemistry builds glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde; direct dehydration of glyceraldehyde yields methylglyoxal; selective keto-reduction gives lactaldehyde; Strecker synthesis with HCN from the formate-formamide pathway produces threonine.

pathways.name: FTT-formaldehyde → formose → glyceraldehyde dehydration → Strecker (NH₃+CO HCN)
pathways.description: Same formaldehyde and formose route as P1, but HCN is sourced independently via UV photocatalysis of NH₃ + CO on TiO₂, using hydrothermal CO transported to the surface.

pathways.name: UV-formaldehyde → formose → glyceraldehyde dehydration → Strecker (formamide HCN)
pathways.description: Formaldehyde from UV photocatalytic CO₂ reduction on TiO₂ (independent of hydrothermal FTT); formose chemistry to glyceraldehyde; dehydration to methylglyoxal; Strecker with formamide-derived HCN.

pathways.name: UV-formaldehyde → formose → glyceraldehyde dehydration → Strecker (NH₃+CO HCN)
pathways.description: Fully surface-sourced formaldehyde (UV on TiO₂) and HCN (NH₃+CO photochemistry); formose to glyceraldehyde; dehydration to methylglyoxal; selective reduction and Strecker to threonine.

pathways.name: FTT-formaldehyde → formose → DHA isomerization → Strecker (formamide HCN)
pathways.description: Uses the dihydroxyacetone (DHA) route to methylglyoxal instead of direct glyceraldehyde dehydration. Glyceraldehyde isomerizes to DHA, which eliminates water to form methylglyoxal. FTT formaldehyde and formamide-derived HCN.

pathways.name: FTT-formaldehyde → formose → DHA isomerization → Strecker (NH₃+CO HCN)
pathways.description: DHA route to methylglyoxal with FTT formaldehyde and photochemical HCN (NH₃+CO). Alternative HCN source adds robustness against intermittent formamide supply.

pathways.name: UV-formaldehyde → formose → DHA isomerization → Strecker (formamide HCN)
pathways.description: Entirely surface-derived formaldehyde (UV photocatalysis) with DHA route to methylglyoxal. HCN from formamide thermolysis. Demonstrates full surface-environment self-sufficiency for carbon scaffolding.

pathways.name: UV-formaldehyde → formose → DHA isomerization → Strecker (NH₃+CO HCN)
pathways.description: Both formaldehyde (UV) and HCN (NH₃+CO photochemistry) sourced at the surface, with DHA route to methylglyoxal. Minimal dependence on hydrothermal inputs (only CO for HCN).

pathways.name: Pyruvate → acetaldehyde → glycolaldehyde → formose → glyceraldehyde dehydration → Strecker (formamide HCN)
pathways.description: Pyruvate from CO₂ fixation on Fe⁰ undergoes decarboxylation to acetaldehyde. Surface UV photooxidation on TiO₂ converts acetaldehyde to glycolaldehyde, which enters the formose network via aldol with formaldehyde (from either FTT or UV). Glyceraldehyde dehydrates to methylglyoxal, then Strecker route. This pathway uniquely connects Fe⁰-catalyzed C3 metabolism to the formose sugar network.

pathways.name: Pyruvate → acetaldehyde → glycolaldehyde → formose → DHA → Strecker (NH₃+CO HCN)
pathways.description: Same pyruvate-acetaldehyde-glycolaldehyde feed as P9, but routes through DHA isomerization to methylglyoxal and uses NH₃+CO photochemical HCN source. Maximizes pathway diversity by combining the pyruvate-derived glycolaldehyde feed with both the DHA methylglyoxal route and the alternative HCN source.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Longest main pathway at 11 steps. The formamide route to HCN adds steps and the formate-to-formamide condensation (rxn_006) is thermodynamically challenging in aqueous conditions. The FTT formaldehyde step and the methylglyoxal-to-lactaldehyde selective reduction are the main chemical weak points.
suggestions: Specify dehydrating mineral surface or thermal cycling for formate-ammonia condensation
Use a metal sulfide catalyst (e.g., FeS/NiS) for selective methylglyoxal reduction instead of bare H₂

pathway_evaluations.feedback: One of the shortest and most direct pathways. Avoids the thermodynamically challenging formate-formamide condensation by using direct UV photocatalytic HCN synthesis from NH₃+CO. The main bottleneck remains the selective keto-reduction of methylglyoxal (rxn_016). Environmental flow is clean: Hydrothermal CO₂→CO→CH₂O, Surface formose + photocatalytic HCN, Biochemical Strecker.
suggestions: Specify metal sulfide or native iron catalyst for rxn_016
Consider adding borate minerals to stabilize formose intermediates in rxn_010/rxn_012

pathway_evaluations.feedback: UV-photocatalytic formaldehyde source avoids the less-certain FTT step but introduces the thermodynamically unfavorable formamide route. The mix of hydrothermal formate generation feeding into surface formamide synthesis is plausible but adds complexity.
suggestions: Consider wet-dry cycling conditions for rxn_006

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Ties with P2 as the best pathway. All formaldehyde comes from UV photocatalysis (TiO₂), HCN from NH₃+CO photochemistry. Both are Surface reactions, which means less dependence on hydrothermal transport for the carbon skeleton. Only CO generation (rxn_002) is hydrothermal. Clean environmental logic.
suggestions: Explicitly state mineral catalyst and conditions for methylglyoxal selective reduction

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The DHA isomerization route adds two steps (rxn_014, rxn_015) relative to direct dehydration (rxn_013) and both routes converge on the same intermediate (methylglyoxal). The extra steps are chemically valid but add no strategic advantage. Combined with the weaker formamide HCN route, this is less efficient.
suggestions: The DHA route provides redundancy but the additional steps reduce overall yield

pathway_evaluations.feedback: DHA isomerization route with the better NH₃+CO HCN source. Chemically sound but unnecessarily long compared to P2.
suggestions: Could be combined with P2 as a redundant methylglyoxal source

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines the UV formaldehyde source, DHA isomerization route, and formamide HCN generation. Too many weaker steps accumulate.
suggestions: Prefer the direct glyceraldehyde dehydration and the NH₃+CO HCN route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The DHA variant of P4. Slightly longer but provides useful redundancy through the isomerization route. All chemistry is plausible.
suggestions: Mainly useful as a backup to P4

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pyruvate-acetaldehyde-glycolaldehyde branch is the weakest part of the network. The UV photooxidation of acetaldehyde to glycolaldehyde (rxn_011) lacks strong prebiotic precedent; radical oxidation more typically yields acetic acid. Also requires a formaldehyde source for rxn_012, meaning this pathway is not truly independent. The longest pathway at 13 steps.
suggestions: Replace rxn_011 with a more established glycolaldehyde source or justify the selectivity of photooxidation
This branch adds convergent diversity but the chemistry is weakest here

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The DHA variant of P9 with the NH₃+CO HCN source. Still bottlenecked by the questionable acetaldehyde photooxidation step. Provides maximal pathway diversity but at the cost of chemical rigor.
suggestions: Consider alternative glycolaldehyde generation from this branch

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed synthesis network for prebiotic threonine synthesis with excellent topology and environmental flow. The core strategy—formaldehyde via formose reaction to glyceraldehyde, conversion to lactaldehyde via methylglyoxal, followed by Strecker synthesis—is chemically sound and aligns with known prebiotic chemistry frameworks. Strengths: (1) Rich convergent topology with 5 genuine convergence points and 10 diverse pathways; (2) Multiple independent sources for key intermediates (formaldehyde, HCN, glycolaldehyde); (3) Clean unidirectional environmental progression from Hydrothermal through Surface to Biochemical. Weaknesses: (1) The selective reduction of methylglyoxal to lactaldehyde (rxn_016) using H₂ is the single greatest vulnerability—the selectivity for keto over aldehyde reduction needs explicit mineral catalyst justification (e.g., ZnS or FeS surfaces); (2) The acetaldehyde photooxidation to glycolaldehyde (rxn_011) lacks strong experimental support; (3) The formate-to-formamide condensation (rxn_006) requires more specific dehydrating conditions; (4) mol_014 (lactaldehyde) is labeled a hub but has degree only 2. Not all Surface reactions specify mineral catalysts. The network would benefit from citing specific experimental literature for the methylglyoxal reduction and including mineral specifications for all surface reactions. Despite these issues, the best pathways (P2, P4) achieve acceptable confidence through well-established formose and Strecker chemistries.
